summary: DHH reflects on how the medium of communication fundamentally shapes the quality of debate, drawing from his recent email exchanges about the Canadian trucker protests. He observes that the same people who would be hostile on Twitter engage in civil, good-faith discussions over email or in person. He attributes this to Twitter's algorithmic optimization and audience dynamics that reward aggression, while one-on-one formats self-correct toward civility. Rather than believing humanity is broken, he argues we're trapped in bad platforms producing bad outcomes, and a change of venue can restore productive discourse.
key_points: The same people behave completely differently on Twitter versus email or in-person — the platform shapes the behavior, not the person
Twitter's audience dynamics and algorithmic optimization incentivize hostility by providing dopamine reinforcement for dunks and attacks
One-on-one communication is self-correcting: hostile tactics cause people to simply stop replying, with no audience to cheer the aggression
The current online political climate obsessively polices who can speak about what, which may be the natural end state of social media as designed
Good-faith debates outside toxic platforms act as seeds that change your perspective of what happens inside them
money_quotes: It's not that there's a new subspecies of homo sapiens inhabiting Twitter, and then another living on email. It's the same people.
We are currently trapped in some bad platforms producing bad outcomes, but the people participating often just need a change of venue for the dark clouds to clear.
You start hurling Twitter dunks at someone on email, and they'll just stop replying. There's no audience to cheer the attack, just the silence of a cut connection, and thus no dopamine reinforcement.
The bigger the topic, the more exhilarating a good-faith debate can be.

Eventually the algorithmic optimization will optimize us all to be these resentful, angry people online. Not even through executive malice, but because these systems run on autopilot when exploiting our all too human flaws.
Every such exchange is a seed planted in your heart. Who knows what'll grow over time.
key_insight: The toxicity of online discourse is a product of platform design, not human nature — the same people who are hostile on Twitter become civil and curious when the venue changes to email or in-person conversation.
